stem: A swimming pool holds 480 gallons and is currently 25% full. A hose fills the pool at a rate of 9 gallons per minute, and a drain simultaneously removes water at a rate of 3 gallons per minute. How many minutes will it take to completely fill the pool with both the hose and drain open?
explanation:
Step 1: Find the current amount of water in the pool. $$0.25 \times 480 = 120 \text{ gallons currently in the pool}$$

Step 2: Find how many gallons are still needed to fill the pool. $$480 - 120 = 360 \text{ gallons remaining}$$

Step 3: Find the net fill rate with both the hose and drain open. $$9 - 3 = 6 \text{ gallons per minute (net)}$$

Step 4: Divide the remaining volume by the net rate. $$\frac{360}{6} = 60 \text{ minutes}$$

distractorRationale.A: Part-to-whole vs part-to-part confusion — the student uses the current water level (120 gallons, which is 25% of 480) as the volume to fill, rather than computing the remaining volume (480 − 120 = 360 gallons). Wrong calculation: $120 \div 6 = 20$ minutes.
distractorRationale.B: Direct vs inverse proportion confusion — the student adds the hose and drain rates instead of subtracting, treating the drain as if it also fills the pool. Wrong calculation: net rate $= 9 + 3 = 12$ gal/min; time $= 360 \div 12 = 30$ minutes.
distractorRationale.C: Correct answer. Remaining water $= 480 - 0.25(480) = 360$ gal; net rate $= 9 - 3 = 6$ gal/min; time $= 360 \div 6 = 60$ min.
distractorRationale.D: Rate applied to wrong quantity — the student correctly computes the net fill rate ($9 - 3 = 6$ gal/min) but applies it to the full pool capacity instead of the remaining volume. Wrong calculation: $480 \div 6 = 80$ minutes.
